questions.questionText: Which drop provides the best approximation of free fall near Earth’s surface?

answerOptions.text: Drop A (smooth metal sphere)
answerOptions.rationale: The streamlined sphere experiences minimal air resistance.

answerOptions.text: Drop B (sphere with parachute)
answerOptions.rationale: The parachute dramatically increases drag, breaking free-fall conditions.

answerOptions.text: Drop C (crumpled paper ball)
answerOptions.rationale: Paper is light and affected by drag, so it deviates from free fall.

answerOptions.text: None of the drops model free fall because gravity varies.
answerOptions.rationale: Gravity is effectively constant over the small height.

questions.passage: A physics class compares motion sensor data for objects dropped from a balcony. Drop A uses a smooth metal sphere, Drop B uses the same sphere with a parachute, and Drop C uses a crumpled paper ball. Students graph velocity vs. time for each drop.

questions.questionText: Why does the velocity-time graph for Drop B level off quickly?

answerOptions.text: The sensor lost connection mid-trial.
answerOptions.rationale: Data show consistent readings, so this is unlikely.

answerOptions.text: The parachute adds drag until net force becomes zero.
answerOptions.rationale: Correct. Terminal velocity occurs when drag equals weight.

answerOptions.text: The mass of the object increases during the fall.
answerOptions.rationale: Mass stays constant; drag is the changing factor.

answerOptions.text: Gravity weakens at higher speeds.
answerOptions.rationale: Gravity is independent of speed at these scales.

questions.passage: A physics class compares motion sensor data for objects dropped from a balcony. Drop A uses a smooth metal sphere, Drop B uses the same sphere with a parachute, and Drop C uses a crumpled paper ball. Students graph velocity vs. time for each drop.

questions.questionText: Based on the pH table image, which pair of substances should both turn litmus paper blue?

answerOptions.text: Soapy water and ammonia
answerOptions.rationale: Both substances have pH values above 7 in the image, so both are bases and turn litmus blue.

answerOptions.text: Orange juice and vinegar
answerOptions.rationale: Those substances are acidic and turn litmus red.

answerOptions.text: Water and vinegar
answerOptions.rationale: Water is neutral and vinegar is acidic.

answerOptions.text: Orange juice and drain cleaner
answerOptions.rationale: One is acidic and the other is strongly basic.

questions.questionText: A student is investigating cellular respiration, the process by which cells convert glucose (sugar) into ATP (energy). The student sets up two test tubes. Both contain yeast and a sugar solution. Test Tube A is sealed to prevent oxygen from entering. Test Tube B is left open to the air. The student observes that the yeast in Test Tube B produces significantly more ATP than the yeast in Test Tube A. Based on the scenario, what can the student conclude about the role of oxygen in cellular respiration?

answerOptions.text: Oxygen is not necessary for cellular respiration.
answerOptions.rationale: The experiment shows that significantly more energy is produced in the presence of oxygen.

answerOptions.text: Cellular respiration is more efficient in the presence of oxygen.
answerOptions.rationale: Correct. The yeast in Test Tube B, which had oxygen, produced more ATP, indicating a more efficient process.

answerOptions.text: Oxygen is a waste product of cellular respiration.
answerOptions.rationale: Carbon dioxide is a waste product of aerobic respiration; oxygen is an input.

answerOptions.text: Yeast can only produce ATP without oxygen.
answerOptions.rationale: The experiment shows yeast can produce ATP with and without oxygen, but it is more effective with it.

questions.questionText: According to the Earth-layer diagram, which layer lies directly beneath the crust?

answerOptions.text: Mantle
answerOptions.rationale: The diagram shows the mantle immediately below the crust.

answerOptions.text: Inner core
answerOptions.rationale: The inner core is at the center of Earth, not beneath the crust.

answerOptions.text: Outer core
answerOptions.rationale: The outer core lies below the mantle.

answerOptions.text: Magnetosphere
answerOptions.rationale: The magnetosphere is not one of the internal layers shown in the diagram.

questions.questionText: Which conclusion is best supported by the Punnett square shown in the passage?

answerOptions.text: All first-generation offspring will be pink because each has the genotype Rr.
answerOptions.rationale: Correct. The passage states that every box in the Punnett square is Rr and that Rr produces pink flowers.

answerOptions.text: Half of the offspring will be red and half will be white.
answerOptions.rationale: That would require two different genotype outcomes, but all four boxes are Rr.

answerOptions.text: All first-generation offspring will be red because red is always dominant.
answerOptions.rationale: The passage describes incomplete dominance, not complete dominance.

answerOptions.text: The cross cannot be predicted because only one parent genotype is known.
answerOptions.rationale: The passage gives the allele each parent contributes and the full Punnett result.

questions.passage: A biology class studies flower color using a Punnett square. One parent flower has red petals and the other has white petals. The square shows that each offspring receives one R allele and one r allele, so all four boxes in the first generation are labeled Rr. In this cross, the heterozygous genotype produces pink flowers because neither allele is completely dominant.

questions.questionText: If students crossed two pink flowers from the first generation, which result would be most likely?

answerOptions.text: A mix of red, pink, and white offspring because each pink parent can contribute either allele.
answerOptions.rationale: Correct. A cross of two heterozygous pink flowers can produce RR, Rr, and rr combinations.

answerOptions.text: Only pink offspring because the first generation was all pink.
answerOptions.rationale: The first generation being all pink does not prevent later generations from showing other genotypes.

answerOptions.text: Only red and white offspring because pink is not a real genotype.
answerOptions.rationale: Pink corresponds to the heterozygous genotype in this inheritance pattern.

answerOptions.text: No offspring with recessive alleles because those disappeared in the first generation.
answerOptions.rationale: The r allele is still present in each Rr offspring.

questions.passage: A biology class studies flower color using a Punnett square. One parent flower has red petals and the other has white petals. The square shows that each offspring receives one R allele and one r allele, so all four boxes in the first generation are labeled Rr. In this cross, the heterozygous genotype produces pink flowers because neither allele is completely dominant.

questions.questionText: Which station should serve as the control when evaluating the effect of fertilizer runoff on dissolved oxygen?

answerOptions.text: Upstream forest station
answerOptions.rationale: It is unaffected by farms or town runoff, so it isolates natural conditions.

answerOptions.text: Midstream near farms
answerOptions.rationale: This is the impacted site, not the control.

answerOptions.text: Downstream near the town outflow
answerOptions.rationale: This location adds new variables (treated wastewater).

answerOptions.text: All stations combined
answerOptions.rationale: Controls must be a single baseline condition.

questions.passage: Students investigate whether increased fertilizer runoff is lowering dissolved oxygen (DO) in a river. They set three sample stations: upstream forest, midstream near farms, and downstream near a town outflow. Each day they record water temperature, nitrates (mg/L), and DO (mg/L). They also note that algae blooms appear most frequently near the farms.

questions.questionText: If nitrates spike midstream while temperature stays constant, which claim best explains a drop in dissolved oxygen?

answerOptions.text: Cooler water upstream dissolves less oxygen.
answerOptions.rationale: Cooler water dissolves more oxygen, not less.

answerOptions.text: Excess nutrients fuel algae that consume oxygen when they decompose.
answerOptions.rationale: Correct. Decomposition of algal blooms lowers DO.

answerOptions.text: Higher river velocity midstream forces oxygen out of solution.
answerOptions.rationale: Faster water usually increases oxygen mixing.

answerOptions.text: Town wastewater dilutes oxygen midstream.
answerOptions.rationale: Town runoff is downstream, not midstream.

questions.passage: Students investigate whether increased fertilizer runoff is lowering dissolved oxygen (DO) in a river. They set three sample stations: upstream forest, midstream near farms, and downstream near a town outflow. Each day they record water temperature, nitrates (mg/L), and DO (mg/L). They also note that algae blooms appear most frequently near the farms.

questions.questionText: The Punnett square shows all four first-generation offspring as Rr. What phenotype should students expect if Rr produces pink flowers?

answerOptions.text: All four offspring should be pink.
answerOptions.rationale: Each box is labeled Rr, and the prompt states that Rr produces pink flowers.

answerOptions.text: Two offspring should be red and two should be white.
answerOptions.rationale: That outcome would require different genotypes in the square.

answerOptions.text: All four offspring should be red because R is present.
answerOptions.rationale: The image models incomplete dominance, not complete dominance.

answerOptions.text: The offspring color cannot be predicted from the image.
answerOptions.rationale: The image provides the genotype for all four offspring.

questions.questionText: Which statement best explains why the Punnett square is useful for this flower-color cross?

answerOptions.text: It organizes the allele combinations each offspring could inherit.
answerOptions.rationale: Punnett squares show the possible genotype outcomes from the parental alleles.

answerOptions.text: It measures how much sunlight each parent plant received.
answerOptions.rationale: The diagram shows inheritance, not environmental measurements.

answerOptions.text: It proves flower color is caused only by fertilizer levels.
answerOptions.rationale: The image models allele combinations, not fertilizer effects.

answerOptions.text: It guarantees the exact number of flowers that will grow.
answerOptions.rationale: Punnett squares predict probabilities and ratios, not exact counts.
